Investment approach

The fund, under normal circumstances, will invest at least 80% of its net assets in equity securities. The fund has no geographic limits on where it may invest. In addition to investing in foreign securities, the fund actively manages its exposure to foreign currencies through the use of forward currency contracts and other currency derivatives.

Commentary as of 09/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.23% (Institutional shares) (MAEGX) and 2.11% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) (MDEGX) for the third quarter of 2025.
  • At a sector level, industrials was the strongest performer. Artificial intelligence (AI) sentiment supported semiconductor and data center-related holdings while the aerospace & defense industry continued to demonstrate robust momentum. The financials, consumer discretionary, and health care sectors detracted the most from relative returns, largely due to stock-specific factors.
  • A key change in positioning was the reduction of the fund’s exposure to luxury goods, most notably the sales of Hermes and Ferrari (RACE). The proceeds were used to fund additional aerospace & defense positions, including in Airbus and Thales. In the information technology sector, the fund exited Synopsys and added Broadcom as a new holding.

Contributors

Industrials holdings performed strongly as Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) continued its impressive turnaround, with particularly strong margins in the civil aerospace division, where the investment thesis is coming through faster than we had anticipated. Vertiv (VRT) benefited from data center expansion as revenues and earnings increased sharply while it raised guidance on all key metrics. Following a challenging 12 months, shares of ASML (ASML) rallied as a key customer agreed a flurry of partnerships.

Detractors

Health care holdings Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) and Masimo (MASI) were the largest detractors from relative performance. Intuitive de-rated, but remained fundamentally strong, with second-quarter earnings per share (EPS) beating the consensus forecast as revenues and margins both came in above expectations. Masimo raised its full-year pretax margin and EPS guidance, though the share price fell as investors interrogated an "incremental value of new contracts" metric. Relative performance was also challenged in the financials sector, partly due to the position in Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY), which fell following its results.

Further insight

We expect volatility to continue given ongoing political uncertainty and the narrow equity market leadership so far this year. In such environments, staying focused on company fundamentals is critical, in our view. The potential for whipsawing is high, though over time share prices are expected to pull to par with business realities, given that earnings and cash flows drive returns over a reasonable period. We aim for our concentrated portfolio to remain diversified, and we find high conviction ideas across multiple areas of the economy, including AI-related industrials and “hyperscalers” (large-scale data center operators), payments, aerospace & defense, and specialized medical technology.

data.title: Western Midstream: How Portfolio Expansion Led To Record Q3 2025 EBITDA
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Western Midstream Partners (WES) is an attractive investment in the midstream sector, with core assets concentrated in the Delaware River Basin. Its growth drivers are expanding its asset portfolio through acquisitions and building a new plant and pipeline, scale in the water sector, improved revenue structure, and expanding its customer base. When compared to its peers, WES has attractive and reasonable financial metrics, validating not only the company's strategic development potential but also its market value growth. Considering the recovery of the global energy market and the growth in demand for electricity in the US, I anticipate an upward trend for these securities, which makes my recommendation “Buy.”

Western Midstream Partners' Strategy And Growth Catalysts

Western Midstream Partners is a company with a diversified portfolio of US energy infrastructure assets, including natural gas, liquefied natural gas, and crude oil facilities. Most recently, the company has been expanding through new projects and the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions (a $1.5 billion deal, while the total value of the enterprise will be up to $2 billion). The strategy provides organic growth, while scaling up operations in the Delaware River Basin. What's worth noting is that Occidental Petroleum, the general partner, is playing a big role in WES's ownership structure. It holds about 43.45% of Western Midstream Partners' total assets.

The investment policy of Western Midstream Partners allows for the expansion of its asset portfolio. The mentioned acquisition of Aris Water Solutions is important for increasing its commercial opportunities in the water supply sector. Previously, WES's asset portfolio included approximately 830 miles of formation water pipelines. Following this acquisition, however, an additional 790 miles of pipelines were added to the company. Additionally, assets in the form of formation water processing capacity of 1.8 million bpd were added. The strategic significance of this deal is related to the expansion of Western Midstream Partners' non-core business. It allows for diversification of the customer base and an increase in free cash flow. Management forecasts that the conclusion of this agreement will increase the share of associated water in EBITDA from 10% to 16% by the end of 2025. It will reduce the risks of dependence on energy supplies, the market volatility of which is the most important threat to the stability of the company's core business.

New projects by Western Midstream Partners include the construction of a new cryogenic plant for natural gas processing (North Loving Train II). The plans were confirmed in the quarterly report for Q2 2025. This facility will have a planned production capacity of 300 Mmcf/d. The expected date of commissioning of this facility is the beginning of Q2 2027.

Additionally, plans to begin construction of the Pathfinder trunkline were announced in Q1 2025. It is strategically important because it will enable the transportation of more than 800,000 barrels of formation water per day to facilities in the Loving County area. The capital costs for Western Midstream Partners to implement this project will be approximately $400-450 million. The project is scheduled to be commissioned in early 2027. Therefore, a significant transformation of WES's business profile is expected during 2027, which may increase the market value of their assets.

Perhaps the key factor for Western Midstream Partners' growth in the foreseeable future is the recovery of the energy market, due to growing demand for electricity in the US and worldwide. The continuing AI boom, requiring the construction of new data centers, has meant an increase in demand for electricity to power these digital infrastructure facilities. Natural gas will account for 42% of electricity generation in the US in 2025. And new data from the EIA shows that electricity demand in the US economy will increase to 4,305 billion kWh in 2026.

As WES is an operator that transports natural gas to facilities where electricity is generated, it therefore has a stable pool of customers who enter into contracts with it for the supply of energy resources. Importantly, the company's main pipeline network is located in the Permian Basin. It is very important because this region is the most developed in the American market for shale oil and natural gas production, which makes it possible to connect producers and sales markets. Besides, WES has access to the transport infrastructure of export hubs located on the Gulf Coast.

Western Midstream Partners Payments And The Intricacies Of Their Taxation

One factor contributing to Western Midstream Partners' high investment attractiveness rating is its payments. Its current forward yield is 9.18% (the sector median is 3.88%). And the growth over the past 5 years was +18.31%. Between Q1 2024 and Q3 2025, payments increased from $0.58 to $0.91.

Because Western Midstream Partners is a Master Limited Partnership (MLP), however, there are special tax rules that apply to the distribution of profits to the partnership's investors. They affect both U.S. residents and non-residents. Distributions to US taxpayers under US tax law do not qualify as dividends. Such distributions are considered capital gains. You should review Schedule K-1 for details on WES distributions, because there may be situations where you are required to pay tax on your share of the MLP's taxable income.

Non-US residents face additional complications when it comes to the taxation of MLP securities. As taxpayers, they have two obligations: US withholding tax and the tax laws of their country of residence. The withholding tax rate is up to 37% of WES payments, since they are considered effectively connected income (ECI). That said, non-resident investors must include the amount of payments received in their tax returns, as they are subject to the domestic tax rate (often fixed). The taxes withheld in the country of origin are generally not tax deductible. This is due to the fact that distributions from MLPs do not qualify as standard dividends.

Therefore, the key advantage of MLPs is that payments are considered capital returns rather than income, enabling reduced taxation for individual investors who are US residents (for non-US residents, there are specifics that limit the benefits of investing in MLP assets). To read more about all the features and tax principles of investing in energy sector MLPs, please follow this link.

Western Midstream Partners' Financial Results For Q3 2025

Western Midstream Partners reported fairly good results for Q3 2025. GAAP EPS was $0.87, and revenue of $952.48 million was slightly below the forecast by $12.04 million. Meanwhile, net income for Q3 2025 was $331.7 million, resulting in record adjusted EBITDA of $633.8 million. The record adjusted EBITDA has been achieved for the second quarter in a row. This outcome was made possible by decisions related to the optimization of operating expenses and prudent resource management. WES also achieved record natural gas processing volumes of 5.5 billion cubic feet per day in the third quarter, up 2% from the previous quarter. More details on Western Midstream Partners' financial results for Q3 2025 are provided below.

Also as part of the conference call on the results of the third quarter reporting, management announced new expectations from the acquisition of Aris assets for Q4 2025. The new forecasts indicate that the new water supply assets will generate additional free cash flow of between $45 million and $50 million. The 2025 adjusted EBITDA forecast is now at the upper end of the range of $2.35 billion to $2.55 billion. See the figure below for more detailed forecast results.

Comparative Assessment Of Western Midstream Partners And Its Competitors

To evaluate the investment attractiveness of Western Midstream Partners, I propose conducting a comparative analysis of key metrics between the company under review and its main competitors. Among them are companies such as Plains All American Pipeline (PAA), Antero Midstream Corporation (AM), Hess Midstream LP (HESM), and MPLX LP Common Units (MPLX). Their selection is due to the fact that they are other major operators in the midstream sector operating in key US production basins (particularly in the Permian Basin). That said, since WES's organizational and legal structure is a Master Limited Partnership (MLP), its competitors are mainly companies with a similar business model.

Let's divide the metrics into three groups. First group of metrics—valuation. It is an important category of indicators that is actively used by investors. The traditional P/E non-GAAP indicator for the last 12 months is 11.40. This is greater than PAA and MLPX (albeit insignificantly), but lower than AM and HESM. Calculating the median for this metric among the five companies analyzed yields a figure of 12.30. Therefore, WES has a discount to the median of 7.3%.

Growth is the second group of comparative metrics. These metrics are more accurate because they show the growth dynamics of key indicators that reflect the economic efficiency of the company's core activities and financial results. The quicker and more confident the business growth, the better the assessment of the potential for share price growth in the foreseeable future. Revenue growth over the past five years averages 5.6%, which is behind almost all companies except AM. The Revenue Growth (FWD) metric, however, is 9.94%, the best among all competitors. In contrast, PAA, which has had the best revenue growth over the past 5 years, shows a negative change (-1.16%). In terms of EBITDA Growth (FWD), WES has a similar advantage. The value is 10.35%, which is much higher than that of its competitors. Whereas in terms of EBITDA Growth (YoY), the situation is reversed. Negative change (-7.17%), whereas all others show growth of around 10%.

Profitability is the third group of comparative metrics. These metrics are important because they reveal how economically efficient a company is in using its resources to generate profit. That is, investors will get an answer to the question: what profit will the company receive from each dollar of sales, assets, or invested capital? The key metric, gross profit margin, is 71.41%. Despite being a very high indicator, it is behind AM and HESM. If you calculate the median metric among the five companies analyzed, you get 59%. So, WES's indicator is above average. By other indicators, Western Midstream Partners has very good results overall. They are behind the leading values of other competitors (sometimes HESM, sometimes AM), but it does not mean that WES securities have a lower investment attractiveness rating.

Turning to the analysis of the comparative growth dynamics of company shares over the past 12 months, it should be noted that Western Midstream Partners securities show a profitability of +11.06%. It is less than the yield of AM (+15.32%) and MPLX (+12.42%), but it has only a slight deviation. Interestingly, PAA and HESM have recorded negative results. WES has increased in value by +262.76% over the past five years, the best result among the assets analyzed. Its closest competitor is AM Securities, which has a return of +179.78%.

Risks Of The Investment Thesis

As my recommendation for WES is “Buy,” risks to the investment idea are directly related to threat factors that could impact the effectiveness of Western Midstream Partners’ business model and operations. My take is that the main risk for WES is market volatility in energy resources, which remains the most critical threat to the financial stability of businesses like this. Additionally, in the latest conference call, management cited weak commodity prices as a risk to the Powder River and DJ basins, which may lead to a reduction in the number of drilling rigs or their relocation in 2026.

Therefore, my rating for WES may be revised if the trend in the energy market changes. Currently, oil prices have stopped falling, whereas natural gas prices are showing seasonal growth. Taking into account forecasts for growth in electricity demand, I think the probability of price risk is lower than before. Due to this, the company's investment attractiveness rating is improving in the foreseeable future (3-6 months).

Conclusion

In summary, the results of the fundamental analysis of Western Midstream Partners allow us to assess the high investment attractiveness of this asset. The key factor for the expected price growth is the expansion of the asset portfolio, where the diversification of products is increasing, enabling the company to improve its revenue structure and adjusted cash flows. Meanwhile, the energy market is expected to recover, driving up oil and natural gas prices and, along with them, hydrocarbon deliveries via the transport infrastructure of midstream companies. Based on the characteristics of WES's acquisitions and investment projects, I anticipate that the main period of growth in their market capitalization will occur closer to 2027. At the same time, the initial impact of the expansion of water supply assets will be felt as early as Q4 2025.

data.title: Alger Dynamic Opportunities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

In the third quarter of 2025, U.S. equity markets extended their upward trajectory with the S&P 500 Index up 8.12%. Strong equity market performance was propelled by a combination of improving economic conditions, solid corporate earnings, and growing expectations for monetary easing. A key driver was the shift in Federal Reserve (Fed) policy outlook: what began as a cautious stance going into the third quarter evolved into a more dovish tone as signs of labor market softening emerged. Fed Chair Powell’s remarks at Jackson Hole reinforced this pivot, and the September 25-basis point rate cut marked the resumption of monetary easing. While uncertainty remained about the pace of additional cuts, the prospect of lower rates supported equity valuations and investor sentiment. At the same time, inflation—though still above the Fed’s 2% target—showed signs of moderation, allowing markets to focus on the likelihood of policy support rather than restrictive measures.

Corporate earnings provided another strong tailwind, as second-quarter results broadly exceeded expectations, and third quarter guidance remained upbeat despite some tariff-related cost pressures. Many firms successfully mitigated these headwinds through pricing strategies and operational efficiencies, while consumer spending stayed firm, buoyed by steady gains in household net worth and greater clarity around trade tariffs. Within the S&P 500 Index, the Information Technology sector outperformed, driven by continued enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI), along with robust demand for cloud and semiconductor products. In contrast, the Consumer Staples sector underperformed the index. While consumer resilience supported market optimism, there was growing acknowledgment that lower-income households are under significant strain, and even higher-income families are feeling pressure, as reflected in increased ‘trade-down’ shopping behavior.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger Dynamic Opportunities Fund underperformed the S&P 500 Index during the third quarter of 2025. For the quarter, the portfolio’s average long exposure was 82.16% and the average short exposure was -33.76%. The portfolio’s average cash exposure was 51.61%. Among long positions, the Information Technology and Industrials sectors were the largest absolute sector weightings, while the Health Care and Industrials sectors were the largest relative sector weightings. During the quarter, long positions, in aggregate, contributed to both absolute and relative performance. Short positions, in aggregate, detracted from both absolute and relative performance.

Long positions AppLovin Corp., Nebius Group N.V., and Talen Energy Corp were among the top contributors to performance.

  • AppLovin (APP) is an advertising technology company offering a digital platform that helps mobile app developers market, monetize, and analyze their apps. We believe the company is experiencing a positive lifecycle change, driven by its AI-powered software engine. While currently focused on mobile gaming, AppLovin is expanding into other market segments. Its Demand Side Platform (DSP) supports ad placements, user acquisition, inventory matching, and performance analytics. Further, we believe AI is central to AppLovin’s growth, driving a large majority of the company’s revenue through its recommendation and targeting engine. In our view, AppLovin gains a competitive advantage, delivering higher-value app installs by leveraging data from its game portfolio and developer partners. We believe this scale and data advantage enhances its network effect, improving its technology and boosting market share in mobile gaming. As developers use the platform, the company collects data that continuously refines its algorithms. Shares contributed positively to performance during the period due to strong operating results, which highlighted continued growth in its advertising platform and software business. Momentum further accelerated in September when AppLovin was added to the S&P 500 Index, a milestone that enhanced visibility and underscored the company’s growing market presence.
  • Nebius (NBIS) is a leading provider of AI infrastructure and cloud-based solutions, delivering specialized GPU computing capabilities designed to support large-scale AI workloads for enterprise and research clients globally. We find Nebius compelling due to its impressive growth trajectory and expanding deployment of advanced Nvidia GPU technology within data centers across Europe and North America, positioning it as a key beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption. During the period, shares contributed positively, aided by continued demand for AI compute and a landmark multi-year agreement with Microsoft. Under this deal, Nebius will supply dedicated GPU infrastructure to power Microsoft’s AI services, marking Nebius’s first long-term contract with a major cloud service provider. We believe this partnership not only validates Nebius’s technology and execution capabilities but also provides a significant revenue anchor, reinforcing its role as a critical player in the global AI infrastructure ecosystem.
  • Talen Energy (TLN) is an independent power producer operating in the PJM market across the Mid-Atlantic and Midwest, where constrained supply and rising demand from data centers running AI workloads—particularly in northern Virginia—are supporting higher power prices and capacity. Talen also leverages its Cumulus digital infrastructure platform, which includes a hyperscale data center campus adjacent to its Susquehanna nuclear facility, providing carbon-free, low-cost energy directly from the plant and creating incremental revenue opportunities. While fiscal second-quarter operating results were modestly below estimates due to extended outages at Susquehanna, management reaffirmed full-year guidance, reinforcing visibility around future demand and grid revenues. The company also hosted an investor day in September, where management introduced its 2027 and 2028 outlook, which served as a positive catalyst for the stock.

Our short position in a company that provides technology consulting, platform and product engineering, and advanced analytics services had a positive impact on portfolio performance. During the quarter the company’s share price declined due to weakening customer demand, along with heightened concerns around a slowdown in IT spending.

Long positions James Hardie Industries, TransDigm Group Incorporated, and Netflix, Inc. were among the top detractors from performance.

  • James Hardie (JHX) is a global leader in exterior building materials, best known for fiber-cement siding, trim and related products across North America, APAC and Europe. The company offers a broad portfolio spanning siding, decking, railing and pergolas under a unified platform. During the quarter, shares declined after management delivered fiscal first-quarter results that disappointed investors and signaled a more cautious outlook. The company highlighted softer housing demand, ongoing affordability challenges, and the need to work through elevated channel inventories.
  • Netflix (NFLX) is a global leader in streaming entertainment, delivering premium video content through a subscription-based platform that now includes an advertising-supported tier and selective live-event programming. We find Netflix to be a compelling investment for its ability to sustain strong engagement at scale, its pricing power, and its expansion into new revenue streams such as advertising and live events. In our view, management’s emphasis on consistent revenue growth and profitability, rather than headline subscriber metrics, supports a more predictable financial profile. During the quarter, shares declined as investors looked past strong fiscal second-quarter results and focused on full year guidance and second-half profitability. Management’s full-year revenue raise was attributed largely to foreign-exchange tailwinds rather than stronger underlying demand, which disappointed expectations following strong year-to-date performance. Moreover, commentary around higher content and marketing investment in the second half of 2025 tempered margin expectations, adding to investor concerns. Despite these near-term headwinds, we believe Netflix remains well positioned due to its global scale, advertising initiatives, and selective expansion into live programming.
  • TransDigm (TDG) is a leading supplier of specialized aerospace components used on many aircraft in service today. The company benefits from a highly recurring business model, with most profits coming from replacement parts that airlines are required to install at regular intervals. Its strong intellectual property and sole-source positions provide significant pricing power, supporting a long history of robust growth and shareholder returns. Over the past 12 months, TransDigm has returned approximately $10 billion to shareholders through two special dividends and share repurchases, underscoring its strong cash generation and commitment to capital returns. Shares detracted during the quarter after results fell short of expectations, driven by slower growth in commercial aftermarket sales and temporary inventory destocking that reduced original equipment demand. While defense sales and margins were better than expected, they were not enough to offset commercial weakness.

Our short position in a clean energy solutions provider negatively impacted performance. We initiated this position due to the company’s significant dependence on government incentives for clean energy and renewable natural gas projects, which are susceptible to potential policy changes in Washington, D.C. During the quarter, the company’s share price appreciated after delivering better-than-expected fiscal second-quarter results, while full-year guidance remained unchanged. Revenue outperformance was largely driven by the “Projects” segment, which tends to be more volatile and less profitable. Management noted that some projects were completed ahead of schedule, contributing to the upside. However, expectations for the second half of the year were more cautious, potentially signaling a slower pace relative to earlier forecasts.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Why 'Diamonds' Are A Trader's Best Friend Here
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Marilyn Monroe may have sung that diamonds are a girl’s best friend. But right now, “Diamonds” are a trader’s favorite! Specifically, the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF (DIA) - once affectionately referred to as the “Dow Diamonds” - is quietly outperforming.

Just look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows the percentage change in the DIA versus the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) since the end of October. The DIA is outshining the QQQ, with a gain of 1.4% versus a loss of 2.2% for the tech-levered ETF (through yesterday afternoon).

(Source: Yahoo Finance)

You can see a similar pattern in S&P 500 sector ETFs. The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) rose 3.2%, and the Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI) gained 0.2% during the same time frame. The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) lost 3.1%.

I’ll get the obvious caveat out of the way here: We’re only talking about a two-week trend. There’s no telling if it’ll last... or if we’ll revert back to a tech-led market before we all dive into our Thanksgiving turkeys.

But consider the logic of the trade here. For one thing, investors tend to look for new leaders at the end of one year and the beginning of the next. For another, the Artificial Intelligence “Boom or Bubble” debate keeps raging. Those forces are likely fueling some rotational action.

The end of the government shutdown could help goose the economy, too, as affected workers get back pay and start spending it. Throw in the potential for new “stimmy” checks - an idea that has been floated in Washington - and you can see why more economically sensitive sectors and stocks could catch a bid.

Long story short? Maybe it’s time to “shop” for DOW diamond-type names in your portfolio this holiday season.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: RA: The Fund Is Paying Out More Than It Earns (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

The concept of investing in 'real assets' sounds like a fascinating idea, but the execution can ruin the appeal. Brookfield Real Assets Income Fund Inc. (RA) provides exposure to a diverse portfolio of these assets, but the execution is flawed so it limits the appeal of the fund. When I previously covered RA, I issued a hold rating due to the interest rate sensitivity and inconsistent earnings. Since the time of my last coverage, RA has released an updated semi-annual report, which has prompted me to reassess the fund's performance, utility, and outlook for the remainder of the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RA's share price has declined by about 2.55%. The fund has essentially failed to participate in the market rally over recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 9.6% over the same period. RA now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 10.7% and issues its payouts on a monthly basis. While this makes the fund an enticing choice for income investors, I remain cautious about the sustainability of payouts.

Additionally, the fund's latest report reveals continued weakness around its earnings and its generous distributions. As a result, the fund has underperformed some notable alternatives that can lead to a higher return or do a better job at preserving capital for shareholders. Even for investors that only care about income generation, there are some risks that should be considered. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that RA implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, RA has total net assets of ~$804M that are spread across 491 different holdings. As the fund's name implies, RA aims to provide an attractive total return through its portfolio of 'real assets,' such as real estate, infrastructure, and natural resources. The fund has a listed objective of providing a high current income and capital appreciation over time. RA has a listed expense ratio of 3.1%, but this is misleading because it includes fees related to operating and interest expenses. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 1%.

Looking at the portfolio by sector, we can see that RA utilizes a mix of Corporate Credit, Securitized Credit, and Equities to generate its earnings. When dissecting the fund's breakdown by sub-sector, we can see that RA is most heavily concentrated on Infrastructure-related securities. The portfolio of securities has a weighted average coupon of 5.33% with an average duration of 3.1 years. The full breakdown of assets is as follows:

  • Infrastructure: 46.4%.
  • Real Estate RMBS: 21.8%.
  • Real Estate: 16.9%.
  • Real Estate CMBS: 5.7%.
  • Natural Resources: 5.8%.

As part of the operating strategy, RA utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 24.58% of gross assets, which I consider to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate means that it can be more costly to utilize leverage, which negatively impacts the operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities. Additionally, investors should consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated.

When looking at the top ten holdings within RA, we can see that many of its holdings are held through different credit structures. However, the fund also maintains exposure to traditional equities, such as PG&E Corporation (PCG), Enbridge Inc. (ENB), and Energy Transfer LP (ET). However, the portfolio has so many holdings that there are no positions that have a weight of more than 1% of the fund's total assets.

Updated Valuation

Since the time of my last coverage, RA released an updated semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.45 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.15 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of 2025, RA is aligned to generate net investment income that aligns with its prior years. This can be an indication that RA's underlying portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income over time. However, the issue is that net investment income hasn't historically been strong enough to generate income that can support distribution on their own.

Therefore, RA is reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge this gap in earnings. The challenge is that net realized gains can only be captured during periods of positive market momentum, which allows RA to sell its securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Looking over prior years, we can see that the level of net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. Therefore, RA offers little downside protection against market declines. As a result of this, RA's NAV has maintained a consistent downtrend over the last decade. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2017: $25.15 per share.
  • 2018: $22.07 per share.
  • 2019: $23.21 per share.
  • 2020: $20.02 per share.
  • 2021: $20.12 per share.
  • 2022: $15.58 per share.
  • 2023: $14.93 per share.
  • 2024: $14.69 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $14.58 per share.

Underperformance Risk

Although RA provides investors with a high starting yield, this shouldn't be confused with the fund's ability to provide high returns over time. Just to demonstrate this concept, I pulled in a performance chart since the fund's inception. As we can see below, RA's share price has declined by nearly 41.8%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 66.9% over the same period. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the fund's returns will come from its ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Additionally, there are some other notable peers that offer investors exposure to 'real assets'. While their underlying selection process in terms of holdings, asset types, and sector exposure may differ, the underlying premise of generating income from a portfolio of real assets remains similar. I believe that two compelling peers are the following:

  • Nuveen Real Asset Income and Growth Fund (JRI)—dividend yield of 11.5%.
  • Virtus Real Asset Income ETF (VRAI)—dividend yield of 5.5%.

Based on the overview below, RA has the largest level of base assets under management. Conversely, VRAI has the lowest level of assets under management of $15.6M while also being the most cost-efficient option since it has the lowest expense ratio of 0.55%. The lower expense ratio can be attributed to the more traditional ETF structure that the fund deploys, unlike JRI, which is also structured as a closed-end fund.

When running a performance comparison against this peer group, we can see that JRI has the highest total return at 69.2%. Additionally, RA has the largest level of share price deterioration, which makes it the least appealing choice in my opinion. Since VRAI's more traditional ETF structure offers a lower dividend yield, the fund underperforms the peer group but does the best job at preserving capital. Therefore, JRI is the best choice for long-term investments that want to maximize total return, while VRAI may be a suitable alternative for investors looking to prioritize capital preservation. For these reasons, it becomes challenging to recommend a position in RA.

Additionally, RA is exposed to the risk of being impacted by borrower defaults in this environment of elevated interest rates. This can be attributed to the exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that are more reliant on debt financing to fuel operations. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 18.8%.
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 63.9%.
  • Unrated: 17.3%.

Questionable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.118 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.7%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.416 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, we can see that RA continues to struggle to generate sufficient earnings that can support the payouts being made. The fact that RA continues to pay out more than it earns contributes to the NAV decay over the last decade. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($0.80) per share.
  • 2021: $2.49 per share.
  • 2022: ($2.15) per share.
  • 2023: $1.50 per share.
  • 2024: $1.18 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.60 per share.

As we can see, RA has only been able to generate earnings that exceed the payouts in two of the last five years, excluding the most recent semi-annual period. Assuming the fund can achieve a similar performance over the remainder of 2025, RA is still unlikely to generate earnings that can support payouts for the year, which may further contribute to the fund's NAV decline.

Therefore, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in RA for investors seeking income stability. The main reason that many investors maintain exposure to a high-yield asset like RA is for the income that it can provide. If that income is under the threat of reduction or directly impacts the fund's capital preservation, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position.

Lastly, the distributions received from RA aren't the most tax-efficient. According to the latest section 19(a) notice, the majority of the payouts YTD were funded by net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or your desired income level, a position in RA would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on RA to a sell at this time. The fund continues to struggle to generate income that can support payouts, which has historically led to consistent NAV deterioration over the last decade. As the NAV continues its decline, so does the share price. This has caused RA's share price to experience a much larger rate of decline compared to some more enticing peers that also provide exposure to real assets. For instance, JRI has outperformed RA in total return while providing a higher yield and doing a better job at preserving capital. Therefore, the overall appeal of the fund is fairly limited at this time.

data.title: XCCC: Limited Benefits For A Potential Risk
data.description:
The BondBloxx CCC Rated USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (XCCC) is an investment vehicle with direct exposure to the riskiest segment of the high-yield universe (corporate bonds rated CCC1–CCC3). This fund is passively managed, replicating the ICE CCC US Cash Pay High Yield Constrained Index, and seeks to offer high returns for a fixed-income ETF (double digits) due to the risk (volatility) it assumes for its target investment universe. From my point of view and based on what we will see below, I recommend keeping the vehicle in your portfolio, if you have it, for the time being. If you don't have it, don't expose yourself to it, as high-yield spreads are sufficiently contained that it is not worth taking the risk of making an incorrect market entry at this time.

Fund Risk Grade

From the image above, we can see that although this ETF is riskier than normal due to the type of bonds it buys, its risk profile on Seeking Alpha appears to be fairly moderate. The turnover ratio and 1-year tracking error are noteworthy. At 49%, and given that this is a passively managed fixed-income ETF that seeks to replicate an index using the resampling method, I think this is a somewhat high ratio for a fund with these characteristics. On the other hand, the 1-year tracking error is 6.36%. While it is true that the volatility of the high-yield universe does not help the resampling strategy used by the fund to accurately track the index, I think that this deviation between the fund's returns and the benchmark is somewhat high for a one-year period.

The fund currently has an expense ratio of 0.40%. Although this is within the industry average, there are other cheaper vehicles for investing in the high-yield bond universe. Readers may wish to consider the Schwab High Yield Bond ETF (SCYB).

Fund Maturities, Credit Ratings & Holdings

The image above shows the breakdown of maturities currently held by the ETF in its portfolio. This snapshot explains, first of all, that the main risk for the ETF is widening credit spreads, rather than interest rate movements. More than half of the portfolio matures within 3-5 years (53.89%), with approximately 24% maturing in the next 3 years and a small portion maturing in more than 7 years. This gives the fund a moderate duration relative to US Treasuries and shifts the investment risk to the refinancing of CCC debt tranches over the next 1-5 years. This means that if financial conditions and risk appetite exist during that time, the fund will perform better than in the opposite case, as companies issuing this type of debt will be able to finance themselves at lower costs due to lower premium requirements from investors.

As mentioned above, the fund focuses almost exclusively on the CCC segment of the high-yield bond universe (junk bonds). Within this segment, it chooses to build a portfolio dominated by the CCC1 (45.37%) and CCC2 (39.59%) sub-tranches. The CCC3 tranche accounts for 13.31%, well below the previous two. The main difference between the three main sub-segments is the credit risk of each tranche. While the CCC1 and CCC2 tranches tend to belong to over-indebted or recently restructured companies, the CCC3 and CC tranches belong to the stressed debt category with a high probability of default and generally with equity conversion clauses in the tranches mentioned (convertible bonds).

The subsectors section shows us the diversification by industry in which the ETF under analysis invests. As we can see in the image, the Media (15.7%), Telecommunications (11.4%), and Healthcare (11.4%) sectors stand out, followed by industrial blocks such as Capital Goods (8.7%) and Technology & Electronics (8.3%). We can interpret this distribution among sectors by saying that the exposure sought by the ETF is based on historically leveraged or cyclical sectors, where primary market discipline and revenue growth are very important.

High Yield Bias

In the image above, we can see the OAS of the high-yield universe (the “extra” that the market demands over Treasuries for assuming credit risk), compared to credit card defaults in the US. We can see that the spread is at very contained levels compared to the credit stress periods of 2008 and 2020. This situation reinforces the thesis I defend in this article, since if you are inside the vehicle, the fund's carry continues to work toward your objectives, but if you are outside, the market timing is not right to consider entering, due to the potential widening of the spread itself. To help the reader understand this better, the market currently pays little for assuming the risk of increasing a new position in this vehicle.

90-day delinquency on revolving credit cards has rebounded from post-pandemic historic lows. It shows a positive trend, with a brief slowdown in the last two months. Historically, when defaults of this type accelerate, credit spreads tend to react with a certain lag, so that delinquencies increase first and then spreads tend to widen, which is dynamite for the fund at this time. Given the current picture, the combination is somewhat uncomfortable for buying today, in my opinion. Delinquencies have been increasing, and spreads are at low levels.

We also use the heat map of adjusted spreads to summarize whether these spreads are expensive or cheap compared to their recent average. Warmer colors indicate greater deviations and, consequently, better entry points. Right now, in most segments, the situation is neutral. There are no signs of widespread tension, so I believe it makes sense to wait for situations of market stress in order to buy the analyzed vehicle at the right time. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Leidos Holdings: Solid Q3 Performance Supports Higher Target
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since I first posted about Leidos (LDOS) in June, the stock has grown by more than 33% — this is one of my best investment ideas this year. The third quarter was also positive, even exceeding analysts' expectations. In all previous articles, my thesis was that stocks are unreasonably cheap compared to peers and their own historical averages. At the same time, the business is of high quality with stable growth in revenue, net profit, and free cash flow, and management pursues an active policy of supporting investors through buybacks and dividends. This thesis persists even after the third quarter – stocks are still undervalued. I maintain the "Buy" rating by updating the target price of the stock to $220 with an upside of about 11%.

Q3 Beats Expectations On Revenue; Backlog Growth Strengthens Outlook

Before analyzing the recently released report, I would like to recall my forecasts for the main financial metrics for the third quarter. I expected revenue of $4.3–4.4 billion, EBITDA margin of 13-14%, and adjusted EPS of $3.0-3.2. I stressed that the risks of not achieving the goals are minimal. And so it turned out.

The revenue turned out to be better than my expectations. The growth was 7% YoY to $4.469 billion. Organic growth was lower by 1%.

Among the segments, National Security & Digital and Defense Systems showed the best results. The demand for the company's services from the defense and intelligence agencies remains high all year, which drives revenue higher and higher. Commercial & International has shown a negative result – there is a temporary pause in new international energy projects. Thus, revenue continues to grow steadily, better than expected.

Q3 book-to-bill, which amounted to 1.3x, can indicate that high demand persists. This means that the company received 30% more orders in the quarter than it managed to fulfill. The volume of funded orders increased by 27% compared to the previous quarter, and the near-term order pipeline amounted to about $69 billion. For comparison, the total revenue for the period 2020-2024 amounted to about $72 billion. All this suggests that the company should have no problems with revenue growth in the coming quarters. I'll give you my forecast in the next chapter, but for now, let's move on to marginality.

Adjusted EBITDA increased, but the margin decreased by 4 bps to 13.8% - again within my forecast. Indeed, I expected margin normalization after reaching the highest margin ever in the second quarter (15.2%). Nevertheless, the profitability of the business still remains high. Net profit was also within the forecast. GAAP diluted EPS increased by 5% YoY to $2.82, while non-GAAP diluted EPS was $3.05 (+4% YoY). EPS growth has outpaced net profit growth due to ongoing buybacks. The net profit itself is only 2% YoY.

In my opinion, the quarter was a success for Leidos. We have reached the upper threshold of forecasts, increased our backlog, are looking for new sources for business growth, and are improving our cost control program to keep margins high. Let's move on to forecasts.

Guidance Raised Again: Momentum Carries Into Q4

Once again, management is revising forecasts upward, which is good news for investors.

Management believes that exceeding the plan for Q3 will help generate revenue in Q4. EPS growth will be affected by an effective tax rate between 23.5% - 24.0% and low-interest costs due to the gradual reduction of the debt burden.

I still think that the new forecasts can be exceeded. According to my model, revenue will be about $17.30 billion, and EPS will be around $11.6–11.85. Adjusted EBITDA will remain above 13%. I am optimistic in my views because, first of all, the huge backlog will clearly support revenue growth. And reducing the debt burden and buying shares from the market will support EPS. Therefore, my forecast is slightly higher than that of the management. Wall Street is also leaning towards more optimistic forecasts than management: consensus expects EPS in the range of $11.49-12.07 and revenue of $17.06-17.39.

Valuation Gap Narrows, But Undervaluation Persists

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030, discounting the forecast FCFs, then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the Market cap. With EV/EBITDA, this is the Enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

My model has not changed since the last article. In short, I expect stable demand for the company's products to remain and revenue to grow by an average of 4.15% per year until 2030. The key factor in the forecast remains the record backlog, which ensures stability and visibility of revenue for years to come. The operating margin will remain close to 10%. This premise is quite conservative, given that Leidos does a lot to optimize costs and increase margins. The company's CapEx is traditionally low and will remain the same – 1% of total revenue. Thus, the average FCF yield will be about 8.2% in the forecast period.

I got the WACC for Leidos at 5.94% due to the low cost of debt and the five-year beta at 0.59x (the prerequisites are shown in the table above). The WACC decreased by 0.40% compared to the September value.

The growth of stocks has more or less leveled the multiples compared to the sector and its five-year averages. However, underestimation of the sector remains significant for almost all multiples. I believe that the quotes, despite the growth, still do not fully reflect the steady growth of the business. In order for the discounts to narrow, the stock needs to grow by another 10-15%. Right now, the multiples are so low, as if investors expect stagnation, declining margins, and market share. This is an absolutely illogical situation.

The reduction of the WACC and the growth of multipliers provide a new assessment for Leidos. In the baseline scenario, both methods are quite close in estimating fair value – an upside of 10.6-11.9% is assumed. The most conservative scenario (WACC 6.9%, EV/EBITDA 11x) gives a downside of 2.3%. Under the most optimistic scenario (WACC 4.9%, P/E 18x), the shares should be worth 22.2% more. Following the basic scenario, I maintain the Buy rating and raise the target price to $220 with an 11% upside.

Healthy Balance Sheet Enables Continued Shareholder Returns

The financial situation of the company has improved. About $711 million of OCF was generated, part of which is used to repay debt, and the other part to pay shareholders. As a result, the debt burden remains controlled – Net Debt/EBITDA is about 1.6x.

In Q3, $102 million worth of shares were repurchased and $51 million in dividends were paid. The Board of Directors raised the quarterly dividend to $0.43 per share (approximately 1% per annum), which is 7.5% higher than the previous level. The share buyback policy is quite flexible – management buys when it sees a correction in stocks, so there were fewer buybacks in the third quarter than in the first two due to the rising share price. Since the beginning of the year, the number of shares outstanding has decreased by 4 million, which has supported EPS growth of almost 3%. In Q4, I expect that the amount of buybacks will not be much higher than in Q3.

Risks

Leidos is most dependent on government spending, which poses risks to the sustainability of revenue growth. Shutdowns, changes in budget priorities, and other problems are hurting the company's business. The competition is quite high, so Leidos misses out on some of the large tenders or sacrifices margins in the fight for the order. The remaining risks are either general macroeconomic in nature (rising interest rates, inflation, etc.) or operational in nature (incidents, cyberattacks, etc.). So far, the company is coping with the risks, and to be honest, I see no reason to be afraid – the order portfolio is well diversified, the backlog provides work for several years ahead.

Final Thoughts

I expect Leidos to continue its growth and business development, and I see that the underestimation persists, even though the discount to peers has narrowed since the summer. The third quarter was pleased with the achievement of the upper threshold of forecasts for almost all financial metrics. Therefore, I maintain the "Buy" rating by updating the target price of the stock to $220 with an upside of about 11%.

data.title: Rates Spark: Dutch Pension Reforms May Be Pushing For Steeper 10s30s
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist | Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Euro rates near their highs again

Euro rates are pushing for new highs, but we don’t think the front end can drive the bearish move much longer. Markets price in only a 30% chance of another cut next year, which seems to be pushing the limit given there are still pockets of weakness in the data and the balance of risks remains tilted towards the downside. From a (geo)political perspective, we can still think of plenty of scenarios that would challenge the growth outlook. Trump’s trade deals don’t seem set in stone, whilst US economic weakness, French politics and the Ukraine conflict also add to the uncertainty.

Meanwhile, the inflation outlook is also still tilted towards disinflation, in our view, which would make a cut more likely than a hike, if anything. And if the ECB were to ease more, then moving in pairs of two cuts would make more sense than just tweaking the rate by a mere 25bp. Having said that, the 2Y inflation swap is at just 1.75% which suggests markets are already positioned for an undershoot of the target. Nevertheless, we will need more data to gain confidence that inflation won’t fall too far.

Dutch pensions fund transition at work in back end steepening

Further out on the curve, the Dutch pension reforms could finally be pushing for a steepening of the 10s30s beyond the US curve. Many investors turned more cautious after the US flattening challenged this popular trade, but with 1 January approaching, we may see a comeback.

In our view, the steepening seen in the 30s50s is a good reason to think pension-related activity is at play here. Recent transition approvals from the Dutch central bank, including headlines this week, reduce the risk of delays. At the same time, the IT side remains complex and could still force delays even in December.

Dutch pension reforms may finally be pushing for a steeper 10s30s

Friday's events and market view

It will be another quiet day in terms of data, as many official releases in the US still face delays despite the government having reopened – the originally scheduled retail sales and PPI for October should still be impacted. More focus will be on Fed speakers after the hawkish tones of late have also pressured front-end rates higher. Schmid and Logan, two hawkish-leaning Fed members, are scheduled to speak today alongside Bostic.

The eurozone will release final October CPI data for Spain and France, as well as the second estimate for third-quarter GDP growth. Among the ECB officials speaking today, chief economist Lane should be the most watched.

There is no government bond supply scheduled for the day. The European sovereign ratings scheduled for review and possible updates after markets close are Cyprus (S&P, A-/Stable), Greece (Fitch, BBB-/Positive) and Portugal (Moody's, A3/Stable).

data.title: Castellum, Inc.: Weak Momentum And Pending Bill Warrants Caution
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am rating Castellum, Inc. (CTM) a hold due to the short-term uncertainties and its weak stock momentum. The company is showing notable progress, as reflected in its backlog in the latest company release. The Q3 2025 backlog stood at $220.959 million, up from $87.565 million in Q3 2024, a 152.3% increase. Fundamentally, this is an optimistic outcome that supports a bullish outlook, given the revenue potential this backlog carries for the company if converted into real cash. While this shows a promising future, I think investors should be cautious as the Senate passes the bill to end the shutdown after a heated fallout. This is the longest shutdown in US history. I think, given Trump’s backing and the Senate’s passage of the bill, the Upper House is likely to pass it in a few days, ending this shutdown.

As it stands, optimism from this backlog improvement and positive developments in ending the shutdown would call for a strong bullish outlook. Still, the exact detail I am looking at here is the backlog that can be converted into revenue (funded backlog). For example, 60.54% of this backlog consists of priced options, which represent contract expectations that have not been funded or authorized. Only 17.74% of the backlog is funded, but even that is subject to the after-effects of the shutdown, such as payment delays after it ends. For example, following historical shutdowns like (2018-2019), it is estimated that contractors are supposed to prepare for around 60-90 days of operating expenses to maintain operations. This is why there is an outlook that the impacts of the shutdown, once it ends, could delay contractors’ payments for months.

While I acknowledge that this improvement in backlog has continued to improve the company’s financial visibility, this is a testament that the company is prepared for a takeoff. This can be seen by its improving financials as well. For example, in Q3 2025, the net income improved from -$0.32 million in Q2 2025 to a profit of $0.42 million. However, uncertainties remain regarding the backlog from interrupted state budgets and mandatory program funding. This headwind is also reflected in the company’s price return, which currently appears muted and in a dominant horizontal movement, a sign of market indecision.

I will now move to a deeper analysis below to unearth why I think the stock warrants a hold for now as we monitor how events develop.

Company Brief

Castellum, Inc. is a cybersecurity, information technology, information warfare, and information operations service provider. These services include program development, strategic planning, intelligence analysis, and cybersecurity support, among others. Castellum serves clients such as the US federal government, healthcare, financial institutions, and data-driven sectors. The company is headquartered in Vienna, Virginia.

A Deep Dive

Castellum is focused on the federal government, suggesting it will leverage the federal government tailwinds that will shape its future performance. Having said that, if you look at the FY2026 discretionary budget request, you note that cybersecurity is still having a reasonable cut.

For example, the Department of Homeland Security’s total cybersecurity budget is almost $2.4 billion. Further, about $642 million will be spent on cyber operations, $244 million on cybersecurity technology and services. Even the Energy Department plans to spend $51.6 billion to support IT investments and modernization, which inevitably need cybersecurity. There is more for cybersecurity in this budget, which gives an optimistic outlook, and this is why I am seeing a good 2026.

Taking a different look at why investors should be cautious, recall that Castellum is focused on the federal government. This means that Castellum will also be affected by the after-effects of the federal government shutdown, including a review of contract conditions and funding. As a result, the existing contracts may continue to operate as expected until the previous appropriations expire. Still, they will not require new financial obligations and will not have access to government facilities until approximately 60-90 days ahead.

Following the near-shutdown end, the new contracts will have to wait for the same period for the new appropriations, extensions, option exercises, and additional funding increments to take effect. This is because the new contracts require additional funding and government oversight, while furloughed employees will also be receiving their payments for the time during the shutdown.

I believe it is worth the wait because the government is actively working on a resolution to end the shutdown within days and extend federal funding for particularly excepted activities that involve protecting property and human life. For example, this active move towards the shutdown includes plans to fund some agencies through fiscal 2026.

Also, since the White House ranks cybersecurity functions as an excepted activity, this provides a reason to hold, as the contract uncertainties resulting from the funding extension up to January 30th, 2026, present a short-term hiccup for Castellum as the company’s excepted activities continue to receive funding. This means that the existing cybersecurity contract Castellum has with the government is likely to continue seeing funding, as the government considers them necessary services to avoid an imminent threat to government property. The government has to continue protecting the security of its information, maintaining electronic records, and maintaining the confidentiality of that information, and here is where Castellum gets some relief.

As it stands, the unfunded backlog and priced options cannot be counted on, as the deal set for the House to pass has short-term funding until January 30th. If full-year appropriations bills are not passed before January 30th, the government could experience another shutdown. However, it is important to note that Castellum also includes a funded backlog, which is subject to uncertainty about whether it will be recognized as revenue, and these uncertainties, in my view, warrant patience. Right now, I rely on “excepted activity” recognition, which the company uses as its primary source of revenue to keep the operations going. Given that the funded backlog rose by a double-digit percentage to 17.74% QoQ in Q3 2025, the expected option will serve the company in the short term.

I recommend that investors keep an eye on the compromise legislation proceedings in the House of Representatives, set to end in days. However, there is still no certainty that the final compromise legislation will allow funding to proceed after January 30th. Ending this shutdown remains a short-term solution as we speak. The shutdown lasted 38 days, the longest in US history, and the bill has not ultimately provided funding certainty post January 30th. This, in my view, is a reason to be cautious.

Financials

The company reported its Q3 2025 results on November 7th, and with the first-ever quarter reporting positive net income of $415,401, there is a reason to be optimistic about Castellum. In the last five years, Castellum’s YoY revenue growth has been on the decline, but the company has recently started growing, with trailing revenue growth of 11.09% to $50.61 million. With the backlog increasing by 152.3% QoQ, this shows strong prospects for this company’s revenue. However, I believe that with the after-effects of the shutdown, I think we need to wait for a consistent upward momentum and clarity regarding the current headwinds before jumping in.

Last year, the company missed its consensus revenue estimate by 19%, and this year, given the significant increase in backlog, the company is likely to beat the FY2025 consensus revenue estimate of $54.51 million. For now, the company is fortunate to be providing essential activities, which will continue to be covered by the federal government post-shutdown under excepted activities funds. This optimism can also be derived from FWD EPS’ significant increase to -$0.04, representing 77.78% growth YoY. However, since this is not sustainable in the long term, the company’s FY2026 EPS is projected to increase by approximately 25%, indicating the possible effects of external headwinds.

The company has not been profitable in its previous quarters since 2021, but after 2023’s deep loss of negative $9.192 million, it has resurfaced with the first net income in Q3 2025 of $415,401. This year’s quarters, the company has beaten the consensus EPS estimate, indicating strong execution. However, given the current potential disruption to operations and backlog fulfillment due to the after-effects of the shutdown, which is nearing its end and revealing a weak funding picture post January 30th, I am cautious.

The Investment Take

Besides the cautious tone I have described above, the stock’s current momentum leads me to a hold recommendation. Over the last three months, the price return is at -4.69%, below the sector’s median of 5.01% and down from its 6-month performance of 23.97%, which was above the sector’s median.

In my view, this weakening momentum is a reflection of the market’s reaction towards the headwinds I have mentioned in this article. Taking it further, the RSI is at 55.5, which is around the neutral region of 50, a sign of market indecision. Above all, both the price and the RSI are trending horizontally, which signals a consolidation phase where a hold decision is justified.

Given this stock performance and the reservations I have, I think investors should wait patiently for the end of the shutdown and clarity on the company’s backlog conversion rate before investing here. Should things turn positive on these two fronts, I anticipate an uptrend to kick in, a time in which I would do follow-up coverage.

Investment Risks

Revenue Concentration Risk

Castellum generates most of its revenue from government contracts driven by its focus on government as a cybersecurity provider and warfare services. Since this is classified as an excepted activity, it highlights the risk of overdependence during a shutdown and the funding delays that follow its end. As a result, the company faces the risk of funding delays, limited contract execution, and policy shifts.

Execution Strain From Limited Operational Flexibility

Castellum’s reliance on accepted activities during shutdown narrows its scope to essential cybersecurity services. As such, the company is limited in diversifying or scaling its operations if federal disruption persists. This exposes the company to bottlenecked margin flexibility and execution capacity when contract timelines flip.

Conclusion

Internally, this company appears to be doing great given its revenue growth rebound in the TTM, profitability, and backlog growth. However, the external regulatory headwind and the shutdown blur this supposed optimism, which is why I am reiterating my hold stance.

data.title: Nebius Group: Buy The Emerging Power Of The AI Cloud
data.description:
Thesis: Explosive Growth and Visionary Leadership

A Company that is in a phase of massive growth and maturity regarding its strategy is Nebius Group (NBIS).

For Q3 2025, revenues reached $146 million, +355% y-o-y. 90% of the company's total revenue comes from its core infrastructure operations.

The Company chooses its deals carefully. As I see it, what it wants is strategic dominance in a rapidly maturing market.

Nebius signed two large deals, with Microsoft and Meta.

The values of the contracts reach $17-19 billion and $3 billion, respectively. With these deals, the Company want to confirm the trust of leading technology companies.

The company has already sold every unit of computing power available. Demand exceeds by far supply, showing the scale of the opportunity. Volozh noted that if there had been more capacity, it would have all been sold.

So the problem is not clients but the speed of infrastructure development. Nebius is at the heart of a huge transition for artificial intelligence.

Investment Acceleration and Solid Financial Strength

The Company is expanding at a rapid pace, and in a few months, it has increased its capacity target from 1 to 2.5 gigawatts by 2026.

By the end of the year, it plans to have 1 gigawatt connected. That means faster physical and digital infrastructure development than any competitor.

CFO Dado Alonso presented a coherent financing plan and capital strategy. The company will raise funds through corporate debt, asset-backed lending, and restricted stock issuance.

Additionally, the Company plans investments for 2025 of more than $5 billion.

Management is disciplined and respectful of shareholders, avoiding excessive dilution. The ARR target is 900 million-1.1 billion in 2025 and 7-9 billion in 2026.

More than half of this target is already considered secured. Nebius is evolving from an infrastructure provider to a complete AI platform.

The plan is with the Aether 3.0 platform to target large enterprises and complex workloads. It offers security, compliance, and control tools in high-demand corporate environments.

At the same time, the Nebius Token Factory opens up new growth areas in the inference market. The service allows companies to transform open models into production systems.

The clients enjoy cost transparency, reliability, and adaptability, and Nebius is not only relying on hardware and infrastructure.

It combines data centers, software, and cloud products in one ecosystem.

The main clientele base includes startups like Cursor and Black Forest Labs, as well as large enterprises adopting AI solutions at scale.

Chief Revenue Officer Marc Boroditsky said that "demand is exceeding supply".

According to him, the Company often says "no" to clients due to limited capacity. Nebius realized that the future of the cloud is not just strength but intelligence,

It is clear that the double investment of hardware and software give advantage against its competitors.

Valuation and Three Realistic Scenarios

Nebius is currently valued at a GAAP P/E of approximately 129 times. This ratio reflects a company in an explosive maturity phase. Earnings are still limited as the company invests in data centers and GPUs. The true value comes from expected cash flow and ARR growth.

The Bull case scenario is that ARR reaches $9 billion by 2026 with an EBITDA margin of 35%.

As net profits increase, forward P/E decreases to 45x, Valuation reaches $40-45 billion, and the indicative share price is almost $200. Nebius is evolving into a "category leader" with a strong moat.

The Base Case scenario is that ARR reaches $7 billion by 2026 with an EBITDA margin of 25%.

P/E stabilizes around 70-80 times. Valuation is estimated at $30 billion, and the share price approaches $150.

This scenario is considered the most realistic and viable and also fits with the diagrammatic analysis that we will see below.

The Bare Case scenario is that ARR remains below 5.5 billion by 2026. EBITDA margin is between 18% and 20%. P/E is limited to 45 times. Valuation falls to 15-17 billion dollars and share price is around 60 dollars. Downside remains manageable, as long as management discipline continues.

Technically, the stock has already gained 380% in the last 12 months and the market has a favorable path in its future course.

The $100 is the entry point after the recent pullback, with $150 being the first target.

Competitive Landscape and Differentiation

Nebius has giants to confront, such as Amazon AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud and Oracle Cloud. Let's not forget how challenging and competitive the market is.

The Company is chasing exactly the same big contracts as the others. Therefore, all of them are investing huge amounts in AI infrastructure and data centers.

This Company stands out as it offers a single solution. The others provide either only computing power or only software.

Nebius combines powerful hardware, its own software, and cloud optimization.

The connection between workloads and GPUs is direct. Thus, Aether 3.0 reduces the cost and delay in inference tasks.

What is also unique is the Token Factory that allows the Companies to convert open-source models into production environments.

Today, in AWS and Azure, this still requires third-party tools.

Finally, Nebius also stands out in the way it finances its development. It has not been overloaded with debt like many others.

Key Downside Risks

Like all companies, Nebius has its risks, and as I see it, execution and timing are the most important ones.

As mentioned above, the Company wants to reach 2.5 GW of capacity by 2026. This means huge projects that don't have the luxury to delay. If construction or GPU deliveries are delayed, revenue will be delayed as well.

There is also the issue of clients concentration. Microsoft and Meta cover the bulk of the contracts. If they change strategy or push for better terms, Nebius will be immediately affected.

Also, the stock is not cheap. It is trading at more than 120x future earnings. Any weakness in revenues or margins could lead to a sharp correction.

Nevertheless, Nebius's unified model and disciplined growth make it one of the few serious propositions in the AI infrastructure market, outside of the big American players.

Conclusion: The Beginning of a Promising Future

Nebius is at the beginning of a long journey.

Its goal is to become the world leader of the AI Ecosystem, while the combination of the technological and financial discipline differentiates it from its competitors.

Its strategy, as far as I am concerned, is realistic and focused on sustainable growth.

If it achieves its 2.5 gigawatt target, it will establish a global presence and stand alongside giants such as Microsoft and Google Cloud.

The expansion of Blackwell technology and the demand for AI are creating huge opportunities.

From my perspective, Nebius deserves a "BUY" recommendation as it has the potential to become one of the most exciting growth stories as it doesn't follow the AI trend. It defines it.
